title: Thoughts on number inputs
content:
## What is a number?

And what is `type="number"`?

`98194`
This is my zip code. It’s a number, no?

`42`
This is my age. Obviously a number!

`13.37`
This is my bank account balance. A number or not?

For a web front-end developer, this question becomes relevant whenever we need to collect information from a user.  HTML input elements allows us to declare what type of input we’re expecting. The type informs the browser, the end-user, and assistive technology how the field should be interacted with.

For example, `type="text"` indicates that we’re expecting any kind of character sequence. `type="date"` indicates we’re expecting a date, and most browsers will render a calendar widget to assist users. And so on. Great stuff! So you’d think that `type="number"` would be appropriate when we’re expecting some kind of number value, like the examples above.

This is where things get kind of weird. You see, in the world of Web, `type="number"`, does in fact not mean “number”. It means “spinbutton”.

*Say what!?*

Yep. That's HTML logic for you.

## Number of things

Basically, it means “number of things” rather than “numeric value”. Kind of. It doesn’t communicate “we expect a number” as much as it communicates “click the arrow buttons to increase or decrease the value”. This is one of those cases where it seems like browser vendors and standards makers talked past each other to the point of just producing a confused mess. TBH.

The "number of things" interpretation makes zero sense for a zip code or currency amount!

And to make matters worse, `type="number"` also includes some arbitrary, behind-the-scenes, state manipulation, which is essentially invisible to consumers. Try typing `1.e` into a number input and check what the value property is.

It’s `‘’`. Yes, empty string. But the field still says `1.e`.

I know, I know, you’re supposed to use `valueAsNumber` on `type="number"` fields. Then the value is `NaN`, which is technically in line with what is displayed. At least if your brain runs on JavaScript.

But in any case, the problem is that it breaks the flow of state. As developers, we’re used to thinking of our state as authoritative to what gets rendered. `type="number"` breaks that expectation. And on the other end of the spectra, this built-in value coercion can also give a false sense of security. Someone might think "`type="number"` already validates the input, so I don’t have to!".

All this can lead to unexpected scenarios, where users end up submitting something other than what they meant. The input field displayed one value, but the actual submission contained another one, and the user didn't realize. Most people will not think "Ah, this is a number input, so my non-valid number is actually `NaN`!". They'll just expect that whatever is in the field is what they're submitting.

## What to do

So what do we do with this?

I think the safest bet is to just avoid `type="number"` in most cases. Only use it when the “spinbutton” meaning makes sense. Like setting number of products in a shopping basket.

Never use it where the integrity of the value is important. Especially do not use it for financial transactions!

Instead, use `type="text"` and `inputmode="numeric"`, coupled with sensible validation and feedback. That way, you avoid all the craziness of the `type="number"` input, but still give a hint of expected input, and touch screen users gets a nice numerical keyboard to enter their number.

That’s it. Happy coding!
